results.title: Perplexity Computer Review: What It Gets Right (and Wrong)
results.content:
Perplexity Computer is genuinely impressive for a certain class of work, and genuinely frustrating for another. It's not really competing with OpenClaw. They serve different people with different tolerances for setup, customization, and cost.

Let me get specific.

## What Perplexity Computer actually is

Perplexity Computer isn't a chatbot with extra features. It's a cloud-based AI agent that orchestrates 19 models simultaneously, routing each subtask to whichever model handles it best. Opus 4.6 does the core reasoning. Gemini handles deep research. Grok picks up lightweight tasks. GPT-5.2 manages long-context work.

You don't configure any of this. [...] You don't configure any of this.

It runs in an isolated Linux sandbox (2 vCPU, 8GB RAM) with Python, Node.js, ffmpeg, and standard Unix tools pre-installed. It has 400+ managed OAuth connectors for services like Slack, Gmail, GitHub, and Notion. It can spawn subagents to parallelize work. It remembers things across sessions. It has 50+ domain-specific skill playbooks it loads on demand.

Available on Perplexity Max at $200/month, it launched February 25, 2026. Think of it as a managed, multi-model AI employee that lives in the cloud.

## What it does really well

Perplexity Computer absolutely nails a few things.

### Cloud environment consistency

results.content:
of Perplexity computer. I'm really excited to show and it could genuinely be super helpful for you. So you can give it a task and walk away and it will work in the cloud for you for hours. Your computer doesn't even have to be on and that is the magic of this Perplexity computer. So this matters specifically if you love CoWork. I personally use CoWork all the time. It's amazing. You could run scheduled tasks with skills. It's great. But since it's running on your computer, your laptop must stay open and if you close the lid, it won't work. you have a scheduled task for a morning brief every morning at 8:00 a.m., you're not going to get it if your computer's closed. But Perplexity computer runs in the cloud so it can actually go and work while you sleep. Even if your computer is closed, it [...] when you walk away from your computer. If you shut your computer, you close Co-work, it can't go off and do the tasks for you. But the thing is with Perplexity Computer, it can do this because it's all running in the cloud. So, one of the biggest key features in Computer is the fact that it has 19 AI models that are working together. So, you have the Claude Opus models for reasoning and routing. You have Gemini for deep research. You have ChatGPT 5.2 for long documents. You have Nano Banana for image generation and V03.1 for video generation. And all with all the new models coming out, this will constantly be updated. So, whatever task you're trying to perform, it'll select the perfect model for it to do for that particular task. So, I want you to think of this like an orchestra. [...] # Perplexity Computer Runs AI Employees 24/7 (Cowork Killer?)

results.title: AI Memory Problem SOLVED 💪 Claude Cowork Projects Review
results.content: frustrating flaw in the early versions of these AI agents, including the first version of Co-work. It's called the cold start problem, and it was a huge roadblock for anyone trying to do real repeatable work. Just imagine this for a second. Your human co-worker comes into the office and you have to reexplain your entire project from square one. You have to resend every single file. You have to go over all the style guides and rules again every single day. Sounds like a nightmare, right? Well, that is exactly what it felt like using an AI without a persistent memory. You were just stuck in this loop. The sources actually have a great name for this, operational amnesia. I love that term. Every single task was its own isolated event. So, you could spend hours on Monday teaching the AI a [...] no, no, this is not just another chat window you open in your browser. Co-work is a full-on desktop app that works directly on your computer with your local files to complete entire tasks. It's all about tackling a whole objective, not just firing back a response to a single prompt. This slide really nails the shift in thinking. For a long time, using AI has been all about mastering the prompt. You had to become a prompt engineer, right? But CWwork flips that on its head. It's built around the outcome. You just tell it what you want, a finished report, an organized folder, and it figures out the steps. This is a tool you delegate to, not just ask questions of. Okay, so this all sounds great, right? But there was a massive really frustrating flaw in the early versions of these AI agents, [...] its own with a full memory of everything it's done before. This is how you really start to get more done. So, it all comes down to this really. We've gone from a tool with amnesia to an AI co-worker that can learn, remember, and build on its experience. The big question isn't what can the AI do anymore. The real question is for you. Now that your AI coworker has a memory, what's the very first thing you're going to teach it?

results.title: Anthropic's Claude Cowork Is an AI Agent That Actually ...
results.content:
Courtesy of Anthropic/Claude

As a software reporter at WIRED, I’ve tested a lot of shitty agents over the past couple of years. These experiences expose a consistent pattern of generative AI startups overpromising and underdelivering when it comes to these “agentic” helpers—programs designed to take control of your computer, performing chores and digital errands to free up your time for more important things. But the bots I installed on my laptop would struggle to complete even basic tasks. They just didn’t work. [...] My first few tries at asking it to archive the emails, instead of deleting them, ran into snags as the bot tripped up attempting to batch archive some promotional emails. As I watched it fail, I pivoted and asked Cowork to go ahead and delete a thousand of those unread messages. Then, the bot clicked around and deleted everything I asked it to … and nothing I didn’t. Even so, the bot messing up is still a real possibility as Anthropic works through bugs and iterates on Cowork. [...] ## Comments
